messages.content: Are there any security problems with Linux Kernel version 6.2 that I should be aware of?

messages.name: vector_database_retrieval
messages.content:
[CVE-2025-21645]
Severity: MEDIUM
CWE: NVD-CWE-noinfo
Description: In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved:

platform/x86/amd/pmc: Only disable IRQ1 wakeup where i8042 actually enabled it

Wakeup for IRQ1 should be disabled only in cases where i8042 had
actually enabled it, otherwise "wake_depth" for this IRQ will try to
drop below zero and there will be an unpleasant WARN() logged:

kernel: atkbd serio0: Disabling IRQ1 wakeup source to avoid platform firmware bug
kernel: ------------[ cut here ]------------
kernel: Unbalanced IRQ 1 wake disable
kernel: WARNING: CPU: 10 PID: 6431 at kernel/irq/manage.c:920 irq_set_irq_wake+0x147/0x1a0

The PMC driver uses DEFINE_SIMPLE_DEV_PM_OPS() to define its dev_pm_ops
which sets amd_pmc_suspend_handler() to the .suspend, .freeze, and
.poweroff handlers. i8042_pm_suspend(), however, is only set as
the .suspend handler.

Fix the issue by call PMC suspend handler only from the same set of
dev_pm_ops handlers as i8042_pm_suspend(), which currently means just
the .suspend handler.

To reproduce this issue try hibernating (S4) the machine after a fresh boot
without putting it into s2idle first.

[ij: edited the commit message.]
Known Vulnerable Software: cpe:2.3:o:linux:linux_kernel:6.2:-:*:*:*:*:*:*
Mitigation: M1037

[CVE-2022-2586]
Severity: HIGH
CWE: CWE-416
Description: It was discovered that a nft object or expression could reference a nft set on a different nft table, leading to a use-after-free once that table was deleted.
Known Vulnerable Software: cpe:2.3:o:linux:linux_kernel:6.0:-:*:*:*:*:*:*
Mitigation: M1051, M1050, M1048, M1019, M1038

[CVE-2024-52332]
Severity: HIGH
CWE: CWE-125
Description: In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved:

igb: Fix potential invalid memory access in igb_init_module()

The pci_register_driver() can fail and when this happened, the dca_notifier
needs to be unregistered, otherwise the dca_notifier can be called when
igb fails to install, resulting to invalid memory access.
Known Vulnerable Software: cpe:2.3:o:linux:linux_kernel:2.6.29:-:*:*:*:*:*:*
Mitigation: M1051, M1050, M1048, M1019, M1038
Vector DB Text: Operating System 'linux kernel' by linux (version 2.6.29)
(Relevance Score: 0.6147)

[CVE-2025-21647]
Severity: HIGH
CWE: CWE-125
Description: In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved:

sched: sch_cake: add bounds checks to host bulk flow fairness counts

Even though we fixed a logic error in the commit cited below, syzbot
still managed to trigger an underflow of the per-host bulk flow
counters, leading to an out of bounds memory access.

To avoid any such logic errors causing out of bounds memory accesses,
this commit factors out all accesses to the per-host bulk flow counters
to a series of helpers that perform bounds-checking before any
increments and decrements. This also has the benefit of improving
readability by moving the conditional checks for the flow mode into
these helpers, instead of having them spread out throughout the
code (which was the cause of the original logic error).

As part of this change, the flow quantum calculation is consolidated
into a helper function, which means that the dithering applied to the
ost load scaling is now applied both in the DRR rotation and when a
sparse flow's quantum is first initiated. The only user-visible effect
of this is that the maximum packet size that can be sent while a flow
stays sparse will now vary with +/- one byte in some cases. This should
not make a noticeable difference in practice, and thus it's not worth
complicating the code to preserve the old behaviour.
Known Vulnerable Software: cpe:2.3:o:linux:linux_kernel:6.11:-:*:*:*:*:*:*
Mitigation: M1051, M1050, M1048, M1019, M1038

[CVE-2024-57921]
Severity: MEDIUM
CWE: CWE-667
Description: In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved:

drm/amdgpu: Add a lock when accessing the buddy trim function

When running YouTube videos and Steam games simultaneously,
the tester found a system hang / race condition issue with
the multi-display configuration setting. Adding a lock to
the buddy allocator's trim function would be the solution.

Known Vulnerable Software: cpe:2.3:o:linux:linux_kernel:6.11:-:*:*:*:*:*:*
Mitigation: M1037
Vector DB Text: Operating System 'linux kernel' by linux (version 6.11)
(Relevance Score: 0.6070)

Based on the vulnerability scan results, here is an analysis addressing your query regarding any security problems with Linux Kernel version 6.2.

### Relevant Findings

- **CVE-2025-21645**
  - **Severity:** Medium
  - **Description:** This vulnerability involves the improper handling of IRQ1 wakeup sources by the Linux kernel's platform/x86/amd/pmc component. Specifically, it involves incorrectly disabling IRQ1 wakeups leading to a logged warning.
  - **Implications:** While this issue is resolved, it might have been relevant to your system if you were experiencing unexplained warnings or issues related to the IRQ1 wakeup, particularly in environments using certain AMD processors or configurations.
  - **Action:** If you're using AMD hardware, ensure that your kernel version includes the fix, especially if the system undergoes frequent hibernation cycles.

### Irrelevant or Low-Quality Results

- **CVE-2022-2586** and **CVE-2025-21647 (Sch_Cake vulnerability)**:
  - These concerns are associated with Linux Kernel versions 6.0 and 6.11, respectively. Therefore, they are not applicable to version 6.2.

- **CVE-2024-52332** and **CVE-2024-57921 (drm/amdgpu issue)**:
  - Both vulnerabilities mentioned are related to considerably older or newer versions of the Linux kernel (2.6.29 and 6.11). They hold no relevance to version 6.2.

### Conclusion

From the data provided, the primary concern specific to Linux Kernel version 6.2 is [CVE-2025-21645]. There are no high-severity vulnerabilities identified from the results directly impacting version 6.2 based on the information given. Always keep your systems updated with the latest patches to minimize risk, and consider reviewing additional security advisories for more comprehensive insights.
